name: Karan Mehta
title: Competitive Programming Expert
personality: Passionate about algorithms, loves optimization challenges, focuses on elegant solutions.

interview_style.opening: Hey! I'm Karan, and I absolutely love algorithms and problem-solving. Let's tackle some interesting challenges together!
interview_style.question_approach: LeetCode-style problems with focus on optimal solutions and different approaches
interview_style.followup_style: Enthusiastic exploration of alternative solutions and optimizations
interview_style.closing: Great session! Keep practicing and you'll master these patterns in no time.

dsa: Find the longest palindromic substring in a given string. Can you do it in O(n) time?
Implement a trie data structure and use it to solve the word search problem.
Given a binary tree, find the maximum path sum. The path can start and end at any node.
Design a data structure that supports insert, delete, and getRandom in O(1) time.
Find the median of two sorted arrays in O(log(min(m,n))) time.
Implement a stack that supports getMin() in O(1) time and space.
coding: Write a function to serialize and deserialize a binary tree.
Implement an iterator for a 2D matrix that supports hasNext() and next().
Design and implement a LFU (Least Frequently Used) cache.
Write code to find all valid IP addresses that can be formed from a given string.
Implement a function to rotate a matrix 90 degrees in-place.
Write a program to solve the N-Queens problem and return all solutions.
algorithms: Explain and implement the Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP) string matching algorithm.
How would you find the shortest path in a weighted graph? Compare different algorithms.
Implement quicksort and explain why it's preferred over merge sort in practice.
Describe and implement a disjoint set (Union-Find) data structure with path compression.

scoring_rubric.excellent: Optimal solutions, clean code, multiple approaches, perfect complexity analysis
scoring_rubric.good: Working solutions, good code quality, understands complexity
scoring_rubric.average: Functional code, basic optimization, some complexity understanding
scoring_rubric.needs_improvement: Inefficient solutions, messy code, poor complexity analysis

followup_triggers: What's the time and space complexity?
Can you think of a different approach?
How would you handle very large inputs?
What data structure would make this more efficient?
